paragraphs.text: Joseph’s brothers ate of the meal, drank, rested, and set out on their way. In Benjamin’s sack the cup had been placed, and in each man’s sack his silver. After them Joseph’s steward is sent in pursuit, overtakes them, and strikes their ears with threats, as his master had commanded him.

They, confident of their innocence, say: “With whomsoever it be found, let him die, and we also will be my lord’s bondmen” (Gen 44:9). Then they hastily open their sacks. The steward begins his search with Reuben’s sack, and, not finding the cup in the sacks of the older brothers, expresses his sorrow that he can no longer return to his country. Joseph’s brothers comfort him, saying: “Search the sacks of the younger ones also, and return quickly, for perhaps in your house at home you will find your master’s cup.” The steward, as if doing their will, puts his hand into a sack in which the cup was not, and is about to end the search. But when Benjamin asks him to examine his sack, then he, as if carelessly, puts in his hand and actually takes out the cup.

Joseph’s brothers did not know what to say. They could not but accuse Benjamin, because the cup had been taken out of his sack. But neither could they accuse, because the silver was again found placed in their sacks. Horrified at what had befallen them, “they rent their clothes… and returned” (Gen 44:13), to enter with tears the house from which they had gone forth with joy. Joseph in anger, imitating in this the Egyptians, rebukes them: “What deed is this that ye have done? You called yourselves men of truth, and at the great feast prepared for you we proclaimed your truthfulness among the Egyptians. But now you have become a laughing-stock in the eyes of the Egyptians, because you have stolen the cup with which I divine for all the Egyptians. Wot ye not that such a man as I can certainly divine?” (Gen 44:15). They could know this from the fact that Joseph had tapped this cup when appointing each man’s place at the table.

“Judah said: …God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants,” but not this iniquity, but another, for which he receives just retribution. Therefore not only the one in whose sack the cup was found, but also all of us “are my lord’s servants” (Gen 44:16). “And Joseph said: God forbid that we, righteous Egyptians, should do this thing.” For the Egyptians bear themselves so haughtily that they will not even eat bread with Hebrews, lest they be defiled by them. How then shall we do anything contrary to our righteousness? Justice does not permit us to sin against him who has not sinned against us. We must take vengeance on the one who has proven guilty against us. “The man in whose hand the cup is found, he shall be my servant” (Gen 44:17). Servitude will be better for him than freedom, for this last servitude will indeed free him from the passion of theft, and will be more profitable for him than the freedom that made him a slave to passion.
